skill.name: sast-semgrep
skill.description: Use Semgrep to plan static application security tests, review findings, map issues to OWASP and CWE, and prepare CI security gates.
skill.summary: Run Semgrep-based SAST workflows with OWASP and CWE guidance.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many dangerous patterns, but review shows they are primarily Semgrep rule examples, vulnerable-code examples, remediation guidance, and documentation references. No malicious intent, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or hidden runtime behavior was found. Publish with a warning that CI examples should pin Semgrep images, actions, and package versions before production use.

medium_findings.title: CI Examples Use Floating Dependencies
medium_findings.description: The CI examples use semgrep/semgrep-action@v1, pip install semgrep, and semgrep/semgrep:latest. These are normal examples, but copied pipelines may change behavior when upstream releases change.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are real CI dependency declarations. They are not malicious, but floating versions are a recognized supply-chain maintenance risk for copied templates.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Substitution Is Local Result Parsing
low_findings.description: The flagged command substitutions read Semgrep JSON output with jq and assign counts to shell variables. I did not find evidence of user-controlled command construction or exfiltration in these snippets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands parse local results.json files and compare numeric counts. No untrusted input is interpolated into executable command names or arguments at these lines.

low_findings.title: Dangerous APIs Appear In Educational Examples
low_findings.description: The eval, exec, pickle, weak crypto, and command execution patterns are presented as vulnerability examples, Semgrep rules, or remediation guidance. They are not executed by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding headings identify these snippets as detection patterns, vulnerable examples, or rule examples. There is no active script file invoking these APIs.

low_findings.title: Secret And Environment Access Is Rule Content
low_findings.description: The os.getenv and secret keyword detections occur in a hard-coded secret detection rule template. The text teaches users to detect or remediate secrets, not to collect environment variables.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The location is inside a Semgrep rule example named example-hardcoded-secret. It references environment variables as a remediation pattern and does not read real secrets.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to Semgrep, OWASP, CWE, and SANS documentation. I did not find evidence that the skill makes network requests to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in references and rule metadata. No fetch, curl, or HTTP execution flow is present at the cited locations.

low_findings.title: Hidden Directory Patterns Are Scan Exclusions
low_findings.description: The hidden path detections are .venv and .git entries in a Semgrep exclude list. They reduce scan scope and do not read hidden files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The entries are under an exclude section in a configuration example. They prevent scanning those directories rather than accessing them.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dangerous APIs Appear In Educational Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The eval, exec, pickle, weak crypto, and command execution patterns are presented as vulnerability examples, Semgrep rules, or remediation guidance. They are not executed by the skill itself.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding headings identify these snippets as detection patterns, vulnerable examples, or rule examples. There is no active script file invoking these APIs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution Is Local Result Parsing
dangerous_patterns.description: The flagged command substitutions read Semgrep JSON output with jq and assign counts to shell variables. I did not find evidence of user-controlled command construction or exfiltration in these snippets.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands parse local results.json files and compare numeric counts. No untrusted input is interpolated into executable command names or arguments at these lines.

content.value_statement: Security teams need repeatable code review without building rules from scratch. This skill guides Semgrep scans, OWASP and CWE mapping, remediation, and CI integration.
actual_capabilities: Plans Semgrep scans for full repositories, pull requests, and baseline reviews.
Maps findings to OWASP Top 10 and CWE categories using included references.
Provides remediation guidance for injection, XSS, path traversal, deserialization, and weak cryptography.
Helps create custom Semgrep rules from YAML templates.
Supplies CI examples for GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, and Jenkins workflows.
Supports SARIF and JSON reporting concepts for security dashboards.
limitations: It depends on Semgrep being installed or available in CI.
It cannot prove exploitability without human review and testing.
Rule coverage depends on language support and selected rulesets.
CI examples should be pinned and adapted before production use.

use_cases.title: Pull Request Security Gate
use_cases.description: Add Semgrep checks that block high severity findings before merge.

use_cases.title: Security Code Review Triage
use_cases.description: Classify Semgrep findings by severity, OWASP category, and remediation priority.

use_cases.title: Custom Rule Development
use_cases.description: Draft and validate organization-specific rules for recurring insecure patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Basic Scan
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me run a Semgrep security scan on this repository and explain which rulesets to start with.

prompt_templates.title: Triage Findings
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Semgrep findings, group them by risk, map them to OWASP and CWE, and suggest a fix order.

prompt_templates.title: Build a CI Gate
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Semgrep CI gate for pull requests that reports SARIF and fails only on high confidence severe findings.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Custom Rule
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Semgrep rule plan for detecting our unsafe helper pattern, including test cases and false positive controls.

output_examples.input: A team wants first-time SAST coverage for a Python and JavaScript service.
output: A scan command plan for local and CI use.
A triage checklist for high severity findings.

output_examples.input: A pull request introduces Semgrep findings in authentication code.
output: Findings grouped by severity and affected component.
OWASP and CWE mapping for each issue.
Remediation steps suitable for the code owner.

output_examples.input: A security team needs a custom rule for unsafe file reads.
output: Rule design notes with positive and negative match cases.
Testing guidance for sample vulnerable and safe files.

best_practices: Start with high severity findings, then expand coverage after tuning noisy rules.
Pin CI action, image, and package versions before production rollout.
Document accepted false positives with clear owner and expiration dates.
anti_patterns: Do not fail every build on untuned informational findings.
Do not treat Semgrep output as proof of exploitability without review.
Do not copy CI snippets without adapting permissions and version pins.

faq.question: Does this skill run Semgrep by itself?
faq.answer: It provides workflows, commands, rules, and review guidance. Semgrep must be installed or available in your environment.

faq.question: Can it replace manual security review?
faq.answer: No. It helps find and prioritize patterns, but humans must confirm context, impact, and remediation quality.

faq.question: Which standards does it reference?
faq.answer: It references OWASP Top 10, CWE, SANS Top 25, and Semgrep rule registry concepts.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium warning. The dangerous code patterns are documentation examples, but CI dependency pins should be improved.

faq.question: What should I customize first?
faq.answer: Customize rulesets, severity thresholds, ignored paths, SARIF upload settings, and version pins for your CI platform.
